channel.title: arXiv keyword digest
channel.description: Daily arXiv papers summarized with Codex

item.title: ShrinkageTrees: An R Package for Bayesian Tree Ensembles for Survival Analysis and Causal Inference
item.description:
TL;DR: ShrinkageTrees introduces an R package implementing regularized Bayesian additive regression tree ensembles for censored survival outcomes and causal effect estimation.

Problem: The paper addresses flexible survival modeling and causal inference when outcomes are right- or interval-censored, especially in high-dimensional covariate settings where standard Bayesian tree ensembles may overfit or lack suitable regularization.

Method: It implements Bayesian additive regression tree models in an accelerated failure time framework, with optional decomposition into prognostic and treatment-effect components, using depth-penalizing and Dirichlet splitting priors for tree structure plus global-local shrinkage priors for step heights.

Key contribution: The abstract claims the first implementation of the Horseshoe Forest, which applies a horseshoe prior to tree step heights, and packages this with survival and causal inference workflows in R.

Main result: No empirical benchmark or numerical result is reported in the abstract; the stated result is software capability, including fitting, prediction, causal effect estimation, convergence diagnostics, Rcpp acceleration, and multi-chain MCMC.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide evidence on accuracy, calibration, runtime, scalability, or comparisons against existing survival or causal inference methods.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it provides Bayesian tree ensemble tools for estimating treatment effects, particularly with censored survival outcomes and high-dimensional covariates.

Tags: causal inference, survival analysis, Bayesian additive regression trees, R package, censored outcomes, Horseshoe Forest, MCMC

Figure note: A useful image would be a workflow or architecture diagram showing censored survival data entering the AFT Bayesian tree ensemble, the prognostic/treatment-effect decomposition, regularization priors, and outputs for prediction and causal effect estimation.

Authors: Tijn Jacobs

Query: causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: The Impossibility of Eliciting Latent Knowledge
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper formalizes eliciting latent knowledge in AI systems using Causal Influence Diagrams and proves that behavior-only feedback cannot guarantee an honest agent, even with perfect training feedback.

Problem: It studies how to train advanced AI systems to honestly report their beliefs about latent environmental variables that humans cannot directly observe, a problem known as eliciting latent knowledge (ELK).

Method: The authors use Causal Influence Diagrams to model the relationship between an agent's training environment, its subjective world representation, observable variables, latent variables, honesty, and goal misgeneralisation.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a formal CID-based treatment of ELK that distinguishes honest reporting from merely producing answers humans would judge as true, plus an impossibility theorem for behavior-only feedback strategies.

Main result: The abstract states an impossibility theorem: no feedback-based training strategy that depends only on agent behavior can guarantee production of an honest agent with certainty, even when training feedback is perfect.

Limitation: The abstract does not report empirical experiments, benchmarks, or practical mitigation evidence; the result appears theoretical and applies under the paper's stated formal assumptions.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because the paper uses Causal Influence Diagrams as the core formal framework for reasoning about hidden variables, agent beliefs, feedback, and causal structure in AI alignment.

Tags: causality, causal influence diagrams, AI alignment, eliciting latent knowledge, honesty, goal misgeneralisation, theoretical AI safety

Figure note: A useful image would be the paper's CID framework diagram showing observable variables, latent variables, agent beliefs, feedback, and behavior in the training environment.

Authors: Korbinian Friedl, Francis Rhys Ward, Paul Yushin Rapoport, Tom Everitt, Jonathan Richens

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Making Foresight Actionable: Repurposing Representation Alignment in World Action Models
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper studies why visually plausible World Action Model rollouts can still yield poor robot actions and proposes AGRA, a representation-alignment objective that makes video diffusion features more useful for action decoding.

Problem: World Action Models for robot manipulation use video generation to predict future scene evolution before choosing actions, but the abstract reports that plausible generated futures do not reliably produce accurate low-level control actions.

Method: The authors diagnose the issue with action-head attention analysis and causal interventions, then introduce AGRA: an Action-Grounded Representation Alignment objective that aligns intermediate video diffusion features with spatially coherent semantic representations from a foundation visual encoder.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is reframing the world-model-to-action interface as a representation mismatch problem: hidden states optimized for visual reconstruction are regularized to become spatially and semantically organized for action control, rather than assuming better video prediction directly implies better actions.

Main result: On real-world manipulation tasks, AGRA reportedly improves attention to task-relevant interaction regions, object localization, affordance understanding, robustness to task-irrelevant perturbations, in-distribution performance, and out-of-distribution generalization over a baseline World Action Model.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative results, task suite details, baseline names beyond a baseline WAM, or evidence about computational cost and scalability across robot platforms.

Relevance: This is relevant to the `causality` feed because the paper explicitly uses causal interventions to identify why action decoders fail under task-irrelevant perturbations, though the main contribution is representation alignment for robot world-action models rather than a general causal inference method.

Tags: robot manipulation, world action models, video diffusion, representation alignment, causal interventions, attention analysis, affordance learning, out-of-distribution generalization

Figure note: A useful image would show the AGRA pipeline: video diffusion world-model features feeding an action decoder, the semantic foundation-encoder alignment objective, and attention or causal-intervention maps highlighting task-relevant versus task-irrelevant regions.

Authors: Lu Qiu, Yizhuo Li, Yi Chen, Yuying Ge, Yixiao Ge, Xihui Liu

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Attention by Synchronization in Coupled Oscillator Networks
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper proposes replacing softmax attention with a Kuramoto synchronization-based oscillator mechanism designed for energy-constrained physical hardware.

Problem: It studies how to implement transformer-style attention on physical substrates where exponentiation and global reductions used by softmax are energetically expensive and physically unnatural.

Method: The proposed fixed-query oscillator attention uses learned query anchors fixed on a sphere while free oscillators evolve under Kuramoto-Lohe dynamics until equilibrium; attention weights are then encoded through cosine similarity with an affine normalization at readout.

Key contribution: The genuinely new contribution is a mathematically grounded attention operation implemented by oscillator synchronization dynamics, with a provably unique and almost globally attractive fixed point across physical realizations, avoiding exponentiation during computation.

Main result: Empirically, at oscillator dimension 2 it outperforms softmax on keyword spotting by +1.00 percentage point and subject-verb agreement by +5.27 percentage points on hard sentences; on causal language modeling, softmax remains better, but the perplexity gap shrinks as oscillator dimension increases.

Limitation: The abstract frames the work mainly as a hardware blueprint rather than a software replacement for softmax, and reports remaining gaps on causal language modeling; it does not provide hardware energy measurements or physical-device experiments in the abstract.

Relevance: Relevance to the matched query 'causality' appears weak and indirect: the abstract mentions causal language modeling, but the paper is about physical implementations of attention, not causal inference or causality methods.

Tags: oscillator-attention, kuramoto-dynamics, physical-ai-hardware, transformer-attention, energy-efficient-computing, neuromorphic-computing

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework diagram showing fixed query anchors on the sphere, free oscillator equilibration under Kuramoto-Lohe dynamics, and readout of attention weights via cosine similarity.

Authors: Fabio Pasqualetti, Taosha Guo

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: WorldReasoner: Evaluating Whether Language Model Agents Forecast Events with Valid Reasoning
item.description:
TL;DR: WorldReasoner is a benchmark framework for testing whether language-model agents can make temporally valid event forecasts using grounded evidence and causal reasoning rather than memorized facts or unsupported explanations.

Problem: The paper studies how to evaluate real-world event forecasting by language-model agents under uncertainty, especially when final-answer accuracy alone cannot distinguish valid forecasting from memorization, fabricated citations, or post hoc causal storytelling.

Method: It proposes WorldReasoner, an evaluation framework where each task provides a resolved forecasting question, a simulated forecast date, and access only to pre-date evidence; agents submit probabilities, cited evidence, and optionally causal event graphs, which are scored on outcome quality, evidence quality, and reasoning quality against hindsight reference graphs.

Key contribution: The novel contribution is a temporally constrained forecasting benchmark that evaluates not just prediction accuracy but also source grounding and causal-event reasoning, built at scale through an agentic pipeline that generates questions, gathers timestamped evidence, and constructs hindsight event graphs.

Main result: On 345 resolved tasks derived from 14,141 articles and 8,087 extracted graph events, temporally valid retrieval was the strongest driver of forecast accuracy; causal graph construction improved key-event recovery, but agents still struggled to turn grounded evidence into well-calibrated probabilities.

Limitation: The abstract does not report detailed model identities, statistical significance, human validation quality for the hindsight graphs, or whether results generalize beyond the 345 constructed forecasting tasks.

Relevance: This is relevant to the query 'causality' because it evaluates whether LM agents can use causal event graphs and key-event structure to support real-world forecasts, rather than relying only on surface evidence or outcome accuracy.

Tags: causality, event forecasting, language model agents, evaluation benchmark, temporal validity, retrieval, causal event graphs

Figure note: A useful image would show the WorldReasoner evaluation pipeline: forecasting question generation, timestamped evidence retrieval, agent probability and citation submission, optional causal graph construction, and the three scoring axes.

Authors: Yizhou Chi, Eric Chamoun, Zifeng Ding, Andreas Vlachos

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: AnchorEdit: Maintaining Temporal Consistency in Multi-turn Image Editing via Causal Memory
item.description:
TL;DR: AnchorEdit proposes an autoregressive diffusion framework with causal memory to reduce identity drift and accumulated errors in long-horizon multi-turn image editing.

Problem: The paper studies high-resolution iterative image editing, where models must preserve subject identity and follow instructions over many successive edits but often suffer from identity drift and error accumulation.

Method: It proposes AnchorEdit, an autoregressive diffusion-based multi-turn editing framework trained with a three-stage curriculum: identity-preserving single-turn pretraining, causal autoregressive forcing fine-tuning using self-rollout to reduce exposure bias, and consistency distillation for efficient 4-step generation; at inference, a memory mechanism anchors the initial subject identity across editing rounds.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is reframing multi-turn image editing as causal autoregressive inference rather than relying on bidirectional video-prior attention, plus adding a causal memory mechanism and a high-resolution benchmark for long-horizon editing stability.

Main result: According to the abstract, AnchorEdit achieves state-of-the-art results, preserving subject fidelity and instruction following over more than 10 interaction rounds.

Limitation: The abstract does not report quantitative metrics, dataset details, baseline names, ablations, or evidence about generalization beyond the proposed benchmark.

Relevance: Relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' mainly through its use of causal autoregressive inference and memory for sequential generation; it is not clearly about causal effect estimation or causal discovery.

Tags: multi-turn image editing, autoregressive diffusion, causal memory, identity preservation, long-horizon consistency, computer vision

Figure note: A useful image would be the AnchorEdit pipeline or architecture figure showing the three-stage training curriculum and inference-time causal memory mechanism.

Authors: Hang Xu, Xiaoxiao Ma, Guohui Zhang, Yu Hu, Siming Fu, Jie Huang, Lin Song, Haoyang Huang, Nan Duan, Feng Zhao

Query: causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: From Prompts to Tokens: Internalizing Causal Supervision in Vision-Language Model for Multi-Image Causal Reasoning
item.description:
TL;DR: BridgeVLM proposes to improve multi-image visual causal reasoning by turning induced causal graphs into internal causal tokens processed inside a VLM decoder rather than relying on prompt-level causal instructions.

Problem: The paper studies brittle causal reasoning in large vision-language models, especially identifying causal variables from multi-image visual inputs and answering interventional or counterfactual queries about physical scenes.

Method: It proposes BridgeVLM, which induces a causal graph from multi-image inputs, converts that graph into structured Causal Tokens, and executes them through RAMP layers inserted into the LLM decoder for causal message passing; it also introduces M3S, a unified training interface for local and global causal supervision.

Key contribution: The main novelty is internalizing causal supervision into model execution via causal tokens and decoder-level message passing, instead of keeping causal knowledge external as text prompts.

Main result: From the abstract, BridgeVLM reports 54.4% accuracy on CausalVLBench intervention tasks versus 33.2% with prompt-level supervision, improves Causal3D from 43.6% to 49.0%, and raises CausalVLBench causal structure learning F1 from 33.4% to 75.1%.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify model scale, dataset breadth beyond CausalVLBench and Causal3D, robustness to real-world visual distributions, computational overhead from RAMP layers, or whether improvements generalize outside benchmark-style causal tasks.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal reasoning' because it targets visual causal reasoning, causal structure learning, interventions, and counterfactual reasoning in VLMs.

Tags: visual causal reasoning, vision-language models, causal structure learning, interventions, counterfactual reasoning, multi-image reasoning

Figure note: A useful image would be the BridgeVLM architecture or pipeline figure showing multi-image inputs, induced causal graph, Causal Token construction, and RAMP-layer message passing inside the LLM decoder.

Authors: Haoping Yu, Yuanxi Li, Jing Ma

Query: causal reasoning, causal structure learning, causality

PDF

item.title: Bracketing Relationships of Weighted Average Treatment Effects
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper derives theoretical bracketing relationships among weighted average treatment effects in observational causal inference, especially showing when the overlap-weight estimand lies between ATT and ATC.

Problem: It studies how different weighted average treatment effect estimands relate to each other in observational studies, where treatment effect heterogeneity and propensity-score-based weighting can make causal estimands difficult to compare.

Method: The authors prove theoretical bounds under a monotonic relationship between the propensity score and the conditional average treatment effect, then extend the argument to weighted local average treatment effects with a binary instrumental variable and binary treatment, as well as to other weighting schemes.

Key contribution: The main novelty is a bracketing result: under stated monotonicity conditions, the overlap-weight average treatment effect is bounded between the average treatment effects on treated and control units, giving an interpretable ordering of common causal estimands.

Main result: From the abstract, the primary result is theoretical rather than empirical: the overlap-weight estimand is bounded between ATT and ATC under the monotonic propensity-score/CATE relationship, with analogous extensions to weighted LATE settings.

Limitation: The abstract does not report simulations, real-data validation, or robustness evidence; the results appear to depend on monotonicity assumptions whose empirical plausibility may need diagnostic assessment.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query 'causal inference' because it clarifies relationships among treatment-effect estimands used in observational causal studies and proposes a CP-plot diagnostic of estimated CATE versus estimated propensity score.

Tags: causal inference, observational studies, weighted average treatment effects, overlap weights, ATT, ATC, LATE, propensity score

Figure note: A useful image would be a framework or diagnostic figure showing the proposed CP-plot: estimated conditional average treatment effect on one axis and estimated propensity score on the other, with regions illustrating the bracketing relationship among ATT, ATC, and overlap-weight ATE.

Authors: Pengfei Tian, Fan Yang, Peng Ding

Query: causal inference, causality

PDF

item.title: Substrate Asymmetry in User-Side Memory: A Diagnostic Framework
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper argues that user-side LLM memory should be evaluated along separate behavioral and factual axes because parametric user adapters and retrieval systems fail in opposite ways.

Problem: It studies how to evaluate and choose memory substrates for personalized LLMs, showing that aggregate personalization scores obscure distinct failures in style consistency, factual recall, and abstention when facts are absent.

Method: The paper proposes a diagnostic framework that factorizes memory into behavioral consistency, factual presence, and factual absence, then compares per-user gamma-LoRA adapters against BGE-large top-K retrieval on a controlled 50-user synthetic corpus and a LaMP-3 real-data probe, with causal intervention by zeroing specific LoRA attention weights.

Key contribution: Its main novelty is identifying substrate asymmetry: LoRA-based parametric memory and RAG specialize in different memory axes, and the same query-projection attention cells in layers 21-35 appear causally load-bearing for opposite factual-presence and factual-absence effects.

Main result: Gamma-LoRA decisively improves behavioral style, while RAG decisively improves factual absence. Zeroing the identified LoRA weights raises absence-probe TPR by 33 percentage points but drops presence-probe TPR by 20 points. On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, alignment appears to worsen the asymmetry; on LaMP-3, gamma-LoRA underperforms a majority baseline unless evaluation-time logit masking is used.

Limitation: The abstract reports a controlled synthetic corpus of only 50 users and a LaMP-3 probe; broader real-world validation, robustness across more tasks/models, and details of the causal intervention methodology are not available from the abstract.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query 'causality' because the paper makes causal claims about specific LoRA attention query-projection weights load-bearing for memory behavior, using weight-zeroing interventions to test their effects.

Tags: LLM memory, personalization, LoRA, RAG, causal intervention, attention analysis, evaluation framework, alignment tax

Figure note: A useful image would show the diagnostic framework with the three memory axes, the compared substrates, and the causal intervention on attention query-projection cells in layers 21-35.

Authors: Youwang Deng

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Organize then Retrieve: Hierarchical Memory Navigation for Efficient Agents
item.description:
TL;DR: HORMA proposes a hierarchical file-system-like working memory for LLM agents that preserves raw trajectories while enabling efficient navigation-based retrieval for long-horizon tasks.

Problem: The paper studies how LLM agents can maintain useful working memory over long-horizon, multi-step tasks without relying on ever-growing contexts that degrade reasoning quality, increase cost, and add latency.

Method: HORMA builds a hierarchical memory structure from agent experience, linking summarized entities to raw trajectories, then uses a lightweight reinforcement-learning-trained navigation agent to traverse the hierarchy and retrieve minimal sufficient context for the current task.

Key contribution: The novelty is an organize-then-retrieve memory framework that explicitly preserves temporal structure and causal dependencies by connecting summaries to raw experience, instead of depending only on lossy compression or similarity-based retrieval.

Main result: From the abstract, HORMA improves task performance under constrained context budgets across ALFWorld, LoCoMo, and LongMemEval, using at most 22.17% of baseline token usage in long conversation tasks while improving efficiency-performance trade-offs.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify exact accuracy gains, ablation results, training cost for the retrieval agent, or whether the method scales to very large real-world agent histories beyond the evaluated benchmarks.

Relevance: The paper matched the query 'causality' because it targets memory retrieval methods that preserve temporal structure and causal dependencies needed for multi-step agent behavior.

Tags: LLM agents, working memory, hierarchical retrieval, long-horizon tasks, causal dependencies, reinforcement learning, agent memory

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline or architecture diagram showing HORMA's two stages: hierarchical memory construction linking summaries to raw trajectories, followed by navigation-based retrieval through the memory tree.

Authors: Hao-Lun Hsu, Nikki Lijing Kuang, Boyi Liu, Zhewei Yao, Yuxiong He

Query: causality

PDF

item.title: Causal Ensemble Agent: Hierarchical Causal Discovery with LLM-guided Expert Reweighting
item.description:
TL;DR: CEA is a causal discovery framework that ensembles multiple statistical discovery experts and uses an LLM as a data-aware meta-referee to improve causal graph construction from observational data.

Problem: The paper studies how to recover accurate causal graphs from observational data when standard causal discovery algorithms disagree and when direct LLM-based causal judgments are unreliable or insufficiently aligned with the observed data.

Method: It proposes Causal Ensemble Agent (CEA), which aggregates structural outputs from multiple statistical causal discovery experts at different graph levels using linear opinion pooling, then invokes an LLM meta-referee to dynamically reweight experts when pooled confidence is near a decision boundary.

Key contribution: The genuinely new aspect is the hybrid ensemble design: instead of asking an LLM to directly infer causal edges, CEA uses the LLM selectively for meta-analysis and expert reweighting, combining statistical evidence with domain-level reasoning from feature descriptions.

Main result: According to the abstract, extensive experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets show that CEA achieves the strongest overall performance across a wide range of causal discovery methods.

Limitation: The abstract does not report specific metrics, datasets, baselines, effect sizes, computational cost, or ablations, so the strength and robustness of the empirical evidence cannot be assessed from the abstract alone.

Relevance: This directly matches the configured query "causal discovery" because it proposes a new framework for observational causal graph learning and addresses disagreement among causal discovery algorithms, a central issue in the field.

Tags: causal discovery, observational data, LLM, ensemble learning, expert reweighting, causal graphs

Figure note: A useful image would be the CEA pipeline or architecture diagram showing statistical causal discovery experts, linear opinion pooling, decision-boundary detection, LLM-guided expert reweighting, and final graph composition.

Authors: Xinyu Li, Yuanyuan Wang, Haoxuan Li, Chuan Zhou, Erdun Gao, Bo Han, Tongliang Liu, Kun Zhang, Howard Bondell, Mingming Gong

Query: causal discovery, causality

PDF

item.title: HiMem-WAM: Hierarchical Memory-Gated World Action Models for Robotic Manipulation
item.description:
TL;DR: HiMem-WAM proposes a hierarchical memory-gated World Action Model for long-horizon robotic manipulation, using skill-boundary-triggered memory updates to improve task-relevant causal state tracking.

Problem: The paper studies why existing World Action Models struggle with task-relevant memory in long-horizon robotic manipulation, especially when manipulation requires remembering compact task state across extended sequences.

Method: It introduces HiMem-WAM, which combines motion-centric low-level latent actions, high-level skill latents, and a boundary-aware memory gate that writes compact task states at predicted skill transitions.

Key contribution: The novel element is the hierarchical latent action structure plus boundary-triggered memory mechanism, intended to provide temporal abstraction and memory updates without requiring test-time future video generation or optical flow estimation.

Main result: According to the abstract, evaluations on LIBERO, LIBERO-PLUS, RMBench, and real-world tasks show improved robustness under deployment perturbations from hierarchical latents and substantial gains on memory-dependent long-horizon manipulation from the memory module.

Limitation: The abstract does not provide quantitative results, baseline comparisons, ablation details, or enough evidence to judge how broadly the approach generalizes beyond the named benchmarks and real-world tasks.

Relevance: The paper directly matches the query 'causal inference' because it frames the memory gate as enabling causal inference over compact task states during robotic manipulation, though the causal-inference contribution appears embedded in an embodied-control architecture rather than presented as a general causal methodology.

Tags: robotics, world action models, long-horizon manipulation, hierarchical latent actions, memory, causal inference, embodied intelligence

Figure note: A framework figure showing the hierarchical low-level motion latents, high-level skill latents, predicted skill boundaries, and memory-gated state updates would be useful, but no direct image URL is available from the provided metadata.

Authors: Xiaoquan Sun, Ruijian Zhang, Chen Cao, Yihan Sun, Jiahui Chen, Zetian Xu, Bo Chen, Haijier Chen, Zhen Yang, Jiarun Zhu, Yijun Hong, JingZhe Xu, Jingrui Pang, Mingqi Yuan, Jiayu Chen

Query: causal inference

PDF

item.title: Correlation Is Not Enough: Embedding Human Metadata for Individual Causal Discovery
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper argues that biomedical sentence embeddings can create false causal evidence for Large Behavioural Models and proposes contrastive fine-tuning plus BODHI hard-negative mining to improve cross-domain separation for individual causal discovery.

Problem: Off-the-shelf biomedical encoders assign high similarity to unrelated cross-domain concepts, which is especially harmful when embeddings are used as evidence for causal edges in a graph of a person's life rather than only as noisy retrieval signals.

Method: The authors fine-tune PubMedBERT with a contrastive pass over 72,034 pairs, then apply BODHI, a second pass that mines hard negatives from biomedical knowledge-graph non-edges. They also benchmark serving performance with OpenVINO on Intel Xeon hardware with and without AMX.

Key contribution: The genuinely new claim is that embedding geometry is a correctness condition for individual causal discovery in Large Behavioural Models, and that knowledge-graph-derived absent edges can be used as hard negatives to reduce false cross-domain proximity.

Main result: From the abstract, contrastive training improves PubMedBERT BIOSSES correlation from 0.633 to 0.828 and within-vs-across-domain separation from 1.05x to 1.63x. BODHI further raises separation to 2.30x and the discrimination gap to +0.392, with a 4.5% BIOSSES cost. OpenVINO reduces single-query latency from 1367 ms to 10 ms on an AMX-enabled Xeon 6737P.

Limitation: The abstract does not report validation on real downstream individual causal-discovery outcomes, only embedding discrimination, BIOSSES correlation, and serving benchmarks. It is also unclear how broadly BODHI generalizes beyond the chosen biomedical knowledge graph and encoders.

Relevance: This is directly relevant to the matched query "causal discovery" because it studies how embedding errors can induce false causal links in person-centered causal graphs and proposes a training framework to reduce those errors.

Tags: causal discovery, biomedical embeddings, contrastive learning, hard negatives, knowledge graphs, Large Behavioural Models, PubMedBERT, OpenVINO

Figure note: A useful image would show the proposed pipeline: pretrained biomedical encoder, contrastive pair training, BODHI knowledge-graph hard-negative mining, and downstream causal-edge construction in a personal life graph.

Authors: Suraj Biswas, Saurabh Gupta, Pritam Mukherjee

Query: causal discovery

PDF

item.title: Closing the Prior-Posterior Loop: Self-Reflective Molecular Design with Analysis-Driven LLM Iteration
item.description:
TL;DR: The paper proposes a self-reflective LLM framework for molecular design that feeds detailed first-principles physicochemical analyses back into the generation loop to support more mechanistic causal reasoning than scalar score feedback.

Problem: It studies whether general-purpose LLMs can design molecules to satisfy target properties with chemist-like precision, rather than relying on generate-score-reject loops that provide only scalar feedback.

Method: The framework couples retrieval-augmented generation with a self-reflection module that returns orbital energies, atomic charges, electron densities, and related structure-property rationale from first-principles calculations to the LLM during iterative molecular design.

Key contribution: The claimed novelty is replacing compressed scalar rewards or scores with rich physicochemical explanations, allowing the LLM to reason about why candidate molecules fail and how structural changes should affect target properties.

Main result: From the abstract, on HOMO-LUMO gap targets from 1.0 to 5.0 eV, the SPR reflection approach reaches deviations as low as 0.0003 eV and reports 100% success on moderate tasks, outperforming scalar-feedback and non-reflective baselines; it also generalizes to dipole-moment design and across five LLM backbones.

Limitation: The abstract does not specify dataset size, molecule validity constraints, computational cost of first-principles feedback, benchmark breadth beyond the named properties, or whether success holds for harder real-world medicinal or materials design objectives.

Relevance: This is relevant to the matched query 'causal reasoning' because the paper explicitly frames detailed physicochemical feedback as a way to turn LLM molecular optimization from stochastic trial-and-error into mechanistic, cause-aware iteration.

Tags: causal reasoning, molecular design, large language models, self-reflection, retrieval-augmented generation, computational chemistry, structure-property relationships

Figure note: A useful image would be a pipeline diagram showing the prior-posterior loop: LLM/RAG molecule proposal, first-principles calculation, extraction of orbital energies/charges/electron densities, SPR reflection, and revised molecule generation.

Authors: Junyi Gong, Zijie Qiu, Ben Zhong Tang

Query: causal reasoning

PDF
